coredata.dc:title: Excitation-dependent multiple luminescence emission of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for cysteine sensing, bioimaging, and photoluminescent ink applications

coredata.dc:description: The concentration of L-cysteine (Cys), a sulfur-containing amino acid, acts as an indicator and biomarker for various diseases in humans. Therefore, the identification and detection of abnormal concentrations of Cys are critically important for determining human health. In this study, excitation-dependent multiple emission nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) are synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. The prepared NS-CDs exhibit a small particle size, good optical properties with high quantum yield, excellent stability, and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) property. The synthesized NS-CDs display a narrow diameter ranging from 3 to 7 nm with a relatively higher quantum yield (QY) up to 11.4%. Under optimal conditions, these NS-CDs show good selectivity for Cys over other biologically relevant species and even other similar (di)thiols (GSH/DTT). The synthesized NS-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity to Cys, with a low detection limit of 23 nM and a linear detection range from 0 to 10 μM. The NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys are used for cell imaging, showing low toxicity to HCT116 cells and exhibits green fluorescence when observed by confocal microscopy. Furthermore, the controllable and strong photoluminescent emission properties of the NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys are visualized as green photoluminescent ink under UV light illumination. The present study is an attractive and innovative perception into the biomedical applications of NS-CDs with controllable photoluminescence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis and PL spectra of the NS-CDs. Inset: optical photographs of NS-CDs aqueous solution under daylight and UV light irradiation with 365 nm (b) PL spectra of NS-CDs with excitation wavelengths increasing from 300 nm to 500 nm.
(a) FTIR (b) PXRD (c) Raman spectrum of NS-CDs (d-f) TEM, HRTEM, and the size distribution histogram of NS-CDs.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum of the prepared NS-CDs (inset; elemental composition percentage). High-resolution spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c), S2p (d), and O1s (e).

(a) Effects of various biomolecules and metal ions (10 µM) on the PL intensity of the NS-CDs, (b) PL response of NS-CDs with increase the concentration of Cys from 0 to 10 µM, (c) The corresponding photographs of NS-CDs solutions with 10 μM of various biomolecules and metal ions under UV light, (d) The color change under UV light of the NS-CDs in the presence of a various concentration of Cys (0–10 µM), (e) Benesi-Hildebrand plot based on the photoluminescence intensity 1/[P-P0] vs. 1/[Cys] concentrations and (f) Limit of detection (LOD) based on a calibration curve of NS-CDs in the presence of Cys at 513 nm (NS-CDs/Cys).
Confocal images of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys (25 µg/mL) with HCT116 cells, including phase-contrast, fluorescence, and overlay images. Scale bars: 50 μm.
Photographs of different letters under UV irradiation light at 365 nm written directly on filter paper from a sketch pen using NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys as ink.
Schematic diagram of the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of NS-CDs for highly selective detection of Cys.
Comparison with other reported Cys sensors.

head.ce:title: Excitation-dependent multiple luminescence emission of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for cysteine sensing, bioimaging, and photoluminescent ink applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concentration of L-cysteine (Cys), a sulfur-containing amino acid, acts as an indicator and biomarker for various diseases in humans. Therefore, the identification and detection of abnormal concentrations of Cys are critically important for determining human health. In this study, excitation-dependent multiple emission nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) are synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. The prepared NS-CDs exhibit a small particle size, good optical properties with high quantum yield, excellent stability, and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) property. The synthesized NS-CDs display a narrow diameter ranging from 3 to 7 nm with a relatively higher quantum yield (QY) up to 11.4%. Under optimal conditions, these NS-CDs show good selectivity for Cys over other biologically relevant species and even other similar (di)thiols (GSH/DTT). The synthesized NS-CDs show excellent selectivity and sensitivity to Cys, with a low detection limit of 23 nM and a linear detection range from 0 to 10 μM. The NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys are used for cell imaging, showing low toxicity to HCT116 cells and exhibits green fluorescence when observed by confocal microscopy. Furthermore, the controllable and strong photoluminescent emission properties of the NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys are visualized as green photoluminescent ink under UV light illumination. The present study is an attractive and innovative perception into the biomedical applications of NS-CDs with controllable photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest for their promising applications in sensing [1], drug delivery [2], photovoltaic devices [3], photocatalysis [4], fluorescent inks [5], and bio-imaging [6]. In Comparison with inorganic quantum dots [7], organic dyes [8] and carbon dots (CDs) [9] have shown unique properties; such as excellent water dispersibility, good photostability, biocompatibility, eco-friendly, multiple emission, low-cost, and easy to modify [10]. Many researchers have shifted their focus to the controlled synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs, particularly those doped with nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) with designable photoluminescent properties [1,4,7]. Several methods for the synthesis of photoluminescent NS-CDs have been described, including laser ablation [11], microwave irradiation [12], electrochemistry [13], ultrasound [14], and hydrothermal method [15]. Among these methods, the hydrothermal method is an efficient, facile, green, and efficient approach. In the current study, the green photoluminescent CDs co-doped with nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) were readily prepared by a hydrothermal method using 3-amino-4-hydroxybenzenesulfonic acid (3-AHBSA) containing C, N, O, S, H element as the precursor. 3-AHBSA is a low-cost material that reacts with strong oxidizers (mineral acids or bases). More importantly, it is used as a colorant to make dyes, pigments, drugs, textiles, personal care products (cosmetics, tattoo inks, and hair dye), food colorants, and printing inks [16–18]. Owing to the presence of polar groups in the 3-AHBSA molecule, hydrogen bonding is the most common interaction, in which polar functional groups act as donors and acceptors for hydrogen atoms [19]. With the rapid growth of a variety of new printing technologies today, counterfeiting issues are attracting much attention. Fluorescent inks have emerged as one of the materials with high-potential candidates because other materials have low stability, complex preparation, and lack several anti-counterfeiting features [20–22]. The application of fluorescent inks is used as a “security mark” that is invisible in visible light, but becomes apparent when excited with UV radiation of the appropriate wavelength [23,24]. Unlike photoluminescent inks reported in the literature so far, NS-CDs-based inks can be written on almost any type of surface, including glass, polymer, metal, silicon wafer, ceramic, and paper. NS-CDs-based inks may exhibit better smearing than commercially available pens [25].

L-cysteine (Cys), a sulfur-containing amino acid, is one of the small molecular biological thiols that play a key role as an antioxidant in the human body. Cys binds with enzymes and enhances the rigidity of proteins by forming disulfide bonds [26]. An abnormal concentration of Cys can cause serious physiological problems in living systems, such as liver damage, growth retardation, lethargy, and Alzheimer's disease, and so on [27]. Therefore, the concentration of Cys in the living systems needs to be tracked accurately and reliable for early diagnosis [28]. In recent years, several analytical methods for the sensing of Cys have been successfully developed such as, mass spectrometry, gas chromatography, and capillary electrophoresis [29], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [30], electrochemical techniques [31], and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy [32]. However, most of these methods are not convenient as they require expensive equipment and complicated sample pretreatment, complex operations, and relatively poor sensitivity for rapid on-site analysis. Accordingly, the development of advanced analytical techniques for Cys detection is highly desired. Compared with the aforementioned analytical techniques, the fluorescent probe method possesses a series of merits such as high sensitivity, high selectivity, and easy operation. Cys has been observed in different kinds of fluorescent probes including fluorescent small molecules, DNA, and carbon quantum dots [33–35]. Therefore, the detection of Cys concentration is very important in the diagnosis of related diseases. In particular, Cys has been used to explore the effects of short chains linking sulfur atoms compared to other amino acids. To achieve fast, efficient, and inexpensive sensing, it is very important to design and synthesize novel receptors capable of detecting multiple responses. However, it remains a big challenge to achieve sensitive and selective detections of Cys. The dithiols, nucleosides, sugar molecules, and metal ions play an important role in many metabolic processes due to their similar molecular structure and composition. To address this problem, it is highly desirable to explore a fluorescent probe (NS-CD) for selective detection of Cys.

In this study, NS-CDs with a high fluorescence QY value (11.4%) showed superior aqueous stability, stable photoluminescence toward changes in the solution pH, ionic strength, and reaction time. In contrast to the previously reported NS-CDs, these synthesized NS-CDs have excitation-dependent peaks and multiple emission bands. More importantly, NS-CDs exhibit excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Cys, with a LOD of 23 nM. Furthermore, NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys are highly suitable for use in bioimaging and as photoluminescent ink given their excellent optical properties, low cytotoxicity, and high cellular uptake (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, the proposed NS-CDs-based sensing platform has a promising future in the specific and sensitive detection of Cys in biological and clinical applications.

3-Amino-4-hydroxybenzenesulfonic acid was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Adenosine (AdN), cytidine (CtN), guanosine (GuN), uridine (UdN), glutathione (GSH), L-cysteine (Cys), dithiothreitol (DTT), methionine (MET), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), silver chloride (AgCl), copper chloride (CuCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), lead chloride (PbCl2) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2), were purchased from TCI chemicals Co. Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout the study while performing all the experiments.

The molecules were registered with a UV 3220 spectrometer (Optizen) or a spectrometer (HITACHI) F-2700 with a Xe Arc Lamp in ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectra respectively. The pH values of solutions were measured using 720P pH meter (Istek instruments). The FT-IR spectra were recorded using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The diffraction study was conducted using a 5° min−1 scanning speed with a wavelength of 1,5405 Å was performed with a 10-80° range of X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (PANalytic X'Pert Philips, MRD model). Raman spectroscopy was performed to investigate the SERS behaviors of substrates using a Raman (Horiba HR Evolution 800) spectrometer with 532 nm laser wavelength, 48 mW laser power, 1200 gr/mm diffraction grid, and 500 nm spatial resolution at the core research support center for natural products and medical materials, Yeungnam University. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA.) measurements were obtained using Multilab-2000 spectrometer with Al Kα radiation mono-chromatized source. High-resolution and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM and TEM) images were recorded (JEOL JEM-2100) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The specimens used for TEM were prepared by evaporating one drop of the sample solution on lacy grids, followed by drying at room temperature.

Briefly, 0.1 g of 3-amino-4-hydroxybenzenesulfonic acid (3-AHBSA) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and then the mixture was sonicated for 10 min. The thoroughly mixed solution was transferred to an autoclave (50 mL) and heated using a hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 10 h. The reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature after the reaction was complete. Subsequently, the yellow suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm at 25 °C for 15 min using a small-size High-Speed Refrigerated Centrifuge (VS-18000 M; Vision Scientific Co. Ltd) to precipitate large particles and the supernatant was dialyzed (~1000 Da cut-off membrane) with deionized water for 48 h to remove the raw material. The final solid NS-CDs were obtained after vacuum freeze-drying for 48 h and stored at 4 °C for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the photoluminescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The NS-CD solution was diluted to keep the absorption intensity below 0.1 and the excitation wavelength set to 450 nm. After that, solutions for absorption and emission measurements were carried out in a 1 × 1 cm quartz cuvette. Quantum yield (QY) was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer F-7000 (Hitachi). Photoluminescence QY can be calculated using the following equation:
QY =
∫
L
emission
∫
E
solvent
- ∫
E
sample

Here, QY was the quantum yield and Lemission was the photoluminescence emission spectrum of the NS-CDs sample. Esample was the spectrum of light used to excite the sample and Esolvent was the spectrum of light used to excite only the solvent of the sphere, collected using a sphere. The solvent in this experiment was a pH 7.4 buffer.

Several types of interferences have been used as a stock solution for the detection of biomolecules (10 µM; AdN, CtN, GuN, UdN, Cys, GSH, DTT, MET, Glu, Fru, and Suc) and 10 µM of metal ions of Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ in distilled water. A stock solution of prepared NS-CDs (10 μg) was dissolved in 2.0 mL distilled water and used as the required volume for further analysis. Prior to the photoluminescence selectivity measurements, test solution prepared in 1.950 μL of distilled water was mixed with 25 μL of NS-CDs solution followed by the addition of 25 μL of each biomolecule and metal ions into cuvettes with the final volume of 2.0 mL. To analyze the Cys sensitivity, the above-mentioned NS-CDs (25 μL) with 1.950 μL of distilled water were added into different concentrations of Cys (1 to 10 μM). For Cys, the competition experiment procedure also followed the above conditions. Then, the reaction mixture was shaken at room temperature for 5 min. The photoluminescence spectra were measured in the range of 300 to 700 nm at a maximum excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

The prepared NS-CDs aqueous solutions were adjusted to different pH values ranging from 1 to 13 by adding 1 M NaOH and 1 M HCl. NaCl solution at different ionic strengths (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 M) and different time interval from 0 to 10 min. at room temperature. After 5 min, the corresponding photoluminescence spectra of NS-CDs were measured. This experiment was repeated three times, each in triplicates.

The cytotoxic effects of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys were tested against HCT116 cells using a MTT assay. Briefly, a 96-well plate with 1 × 105 cells/mL was cultivated in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then treated with various concentrations (5.0–25.0 µg/mL) of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, 20 μL of a MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to the well and extended the incubation period for 24 h at 37 °C. Over the incubation period, the media was removed, and the formation of purple-colored formazan was dissolved by adding 150 μL DMSO. Cell viability measurements were performed three times. HCT116 cells were allowed to grow on glass coverslip using Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS) in a humidified atmosphere condition with 5% CO2. The cells were then incubated with 25.0 µg/mL of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys for 24 h. To measure the Cys uptake, the NS-CDs-pretreated HCT116 cells were again incubated Cys for 6 h at 37 °C. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader. Cell imaging was examined with a CLSM (IX-81, Olympus, Japan).

The NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys solutions were loaded into a marker pen, and written the letters of “YU YR-Lee” on a filter paper. The letters were observed and taken photos under UV light irradiation (365 nm).

The structure and morphology of the prepared NS-CDs were characterized using the following techniques: UV–Vis, photoluminescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Raman spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectrometry was used to test the optical properties of NS-CDs. In Fig. 1 a, NS-CDs show that a typical absorption peak appeared at 219 nm is assigned to the π–π* transition of C═C and a shoulder peak appeared at around 260 nm due to the π–π* transition of C═N bonds in the sp2 carbon in NS-CDs. In addition, the excitation band at 440 nm is related to the n–π* transition of the C═O defect on the surface of the NS-CDs [36]. Furthermore, NS-CDs showed the maximum excitation and emission peaks at 450 nm and 513 nm, respectively, and NS-CDs quantum yield (QY) was 11.4%. The diluted NS-CD solution was colorless in daylight but turned bright green under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp (insert in Fig. 1a). The resulting shorter wavelength NS-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior from 300 to 320 nm and 330 to 350 nm. This excitation was attributed to the different surface states, including functional groups, which influence the bandgap of NS-CDs (Fig. 1b) [37]. On the other hand, these NS-CDs exhibited strong and stable emission at 513 nm (λexc = 450 nm) and exhibited high photostability and PL behavior independent of excitation from 360 to 500 nm. Usually, excitation‐dependent PL behaviors of NS-CDs reflect effects from particles of different sizes in the sample and distribution of different surface states. Therefore, the excitation‐independent PL behavior suggests that the PL properties of the NS‐CDs depend on the particle size and surface states. The excitation/emission matrix evidenced, in some cases, two distinct PL bands, in the blue and green region, with different excitation dependence, calling for multiple contributions to the emission spectrum [38].

For better stability performance, the experimental conditions, including the pH, ionic strength, and reaction time were optimized. The stability was tested under different pH conditions (1.0 – 13.0). The PL intensity was enhanced when the pH was increased from 1.0 to 7.0, whereas the PL intensity decreased with further increases in pH from 8.0 to 13.0, respectively (Fig. S1a). When the pH was increased from 3 to 9, the NS-CDs gradually showed a more negative charge. This was attributed to the occurrence of surface charge changes by protonation or deprotonation. Furthermore, the aqueous solution of the CDs also exhibited better salt resistance. Sodium chloride (NaCl) solutions with different concentrations in the range of 0 – 1.0 M were added to the NS-CDs solution, and the result showed that the PL intensity of the NS-CDs remained nearly consistent (Fig. S1b). In addition, interestingly, the higher stability of NS-CDs was fast with enhanced PL intensity in less than 2 min. (Fig. S1c).

The FT-IR spectra of NS-CDs revealed a band around 3345 cm−1 and 3201 cm−1 that were assigned to the –OH and –NH stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The major characteristics were the strong and distinct peaks at 1443, 1517, 1583, 2362, and 1213 cm−1, which were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C − N, C = C, C = O, S–H, and C–S–C bonds, indicating the presence of N and S on the NS-CDs surface [37,39]. The NS-CDs exhibits a broad PXRD 2θ peak at approximately 25.7°, which was attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b) [40]. The Raman spectrum showed two broad peaks at approximately 1576.5 and 1361.0 cm−1 (Fig. 2c), corresponding to the G and D bands (sp2 and sp3 hybridized). The G and D bands were accompanied by the vibration of sp2 and sp3 hybridized C-atoms in the hexagonal and 2-D terminal faces of disordered graphites [41]. The relative intensity of D and G bands (ID/IG) is found to be 0.94, indicating the formation of the graphitic nature of NS-CDs. The TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 2d and 2e) showed that the NS-CDs have an average diameter of 4.0 ± 0.23 nm (Fig. 2f) ranging from 3.0 to 7.0 nm, with a crystal distance of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystallographic faces of graphite carbon (inset in Fig. 2e) [36].

XPS analysis was conducted to examine the composition of NS-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the survey spectrum of NS-CDs revealed four obvious peaks mainly consisting of C1s, N1s, S2p, and O1s at a binding energy of approximately 285, 401, 168, and 532 eV, respectively [36]. The elemental composition percentage of C, N, S, and O was 61.69%, 10.79%, 6.66%, and 20.86%, respectively (inset Fig. 3a). The C1s peaks at 288.1, 285.3, and 284.0 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectra were attributed to carbon as C = O, C–O/C–S, and C–C/C = O, respectively (Fig. 3b). The N1s showed three peaks at 401.8, 401.1, and 399.5 eV (Fig. 3c), which were assigned to N–H, N–C, and C–N–C, respectively. The S2p peaks centered at 169.1, 168.5, and 167.8 eV indicated the existence of three forms of sulfur (S = O, S2p1/2, and S2p3/2), as shown in Fig. 3d. The peaks for the O1s spectrum at 531.3, 532.0, and 532.7 eV, were fitted to three maxima corresponding to O = C–O, C–O, and C = O, respectively, which were compatible with the characteristic C = O bond in the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3e). Therefore, the functional groups of the as-prepared NS-CDs characterized by XPS are consistent with FTIR spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Cys detection
ce:section.ce:para: For the photoluminescence (PL) response of NS-CDs, biomolecules (AdN, CtN, GuN, UdN, Cys, GSH, DTT, MET, Glu, Fru, and Suc) and metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) were added to the NS-CDs solution with a final concentration of 10 μM. The PL spectra were recorded with excitation at 450 nm to study the selectivity. The PL spectrum of the NS-CDs dispersion was enhanced significantly by Cys over other bio-thiols (GSH and DTT) (Fig. 4 a). The thiol group of Cys acts as a nucleophile toward the intermediate imine. Based on their reactivity, Cys strong interaction with NS-CDs are significantly enhanced the PL of NS-CDs. In contrast, other biomolecules and metal ions showed little or no change in PL, indicating that the NS-CDs have high selectivity towards Cys [42,43]. Cys enhanced the PL intensity more than other biomolecules and metal ions, as shown in Fig. 4a and 4c. NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys showed an increase in quantum yield from 11.4% to 22.5%, indicating that the PL origin of NS-CDs/Cys is different from that of NS-CDs [43]. On the other hand, PL sensing could also be applied to the detection of Cys. The PL intensity increased when Cys was added. The PL intensity of NS-CDs at 513 nm increased gradually with increasing Cys concentration, suggesting that the NS-CDs solution could be used as a PL probe for Cys (Fig. 4b) [44]. The green emission of NS-CDs changed to a strong yellowish-green emission upon the addition of Cys at various concentrations, as shown in the photographs in Fig. 4b (inset) and 4d.

Furthermore, a plot of the change in PL intensity at 513 nm shows the enhanced efficiency [1/P-P0] versus the concentration of Cys in the range of 0 – 10 μM exhibited a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9961) (Fig. 4e). The linear range and limit of the quantitative detection for Cys (0–10 μM) were found to be 23 nM. It was calculated using the equation: LOD = 3 × SD / S (Fig. 4f) [45]. The results revealed that NS-CDs can detect Cys with good sensitivity and low detection limit values in aqueous media. To further explore and confirm the practical applicability of NS-CDs as a Cys selective sensor, the competition experiments were conducted using biomolecules (10 μM) including the two other bio-thiols (GSH and DTT) and metal ions to which 10 μM Cys were added. As depicted in Fig. S2, it can be seen that the PL intensity of the NS-CDs/Cys sensor does not change significantly even with the addition of other molecules and metal ions. Moreover, the response of the NS-CDs/Cys sensor was not interfered with by any interferences. Many good sensor selectivities for Cys have been reported in the literature as summarized in Table 1
[46–56].

Although these NS-CDs are equivalent or better effective in detecting Cys molecules compared to other systems, they have a small linear dynamic range, which is one of the disadvantages of NS-CD/Cys in biological applications except living cell bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of sensor complex for cellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs are attributed to their practical application. CDs can emit strong fluorescence/photoluminescence and exhibit low cytotoxicity, making them suitable for biological and bio-imaging applications [57]. Before starting the cell imaging experiment, the cell toxicity was first examined using a MTT assay to determine the ability of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys. Different concentrations of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys (5–25 µg/mL) were added to the cultured HCT116 cells and the cell viability was calculated by measuring the OD values after 24 h incubation. As the concentrations of NS-CD and NS-CD/Cys were varied, HCT116 cell viability was 92.3% and 99.2% or more in the 25 µg/mL concentration range, which indicated that NS-CDs/Cys were less toxic than NS-CDs as shown in Fig. S3 [58]. Therefore, it can be said that prepared NS-CDs/Cys sensor assembly is the best approach for the sensing and imaging applications of endogenous Cys with acceptable cytotoxicity at 25 µg/mL.

However, after exposing HCT116 cells to 10 μM Cys, the fluorescence of NS-CDs increased, demonstrating that Cys could be detected in living cells after 24 h treatment, including phase contrast, fluorescence, and overlay images (Fig. 5 ) [59]. The phase-contrast transmission of HCT116 cells shows a clear image of the cells. Significant changes in green to yellow-green emission of NS-CD and NS-CD/Cys-treated cells confirmed the high selectivity of the sensor complex in Cys detection in HCT116 cells, which provided clear evidence of fluorescence enhanced in live-cell imaging by confocal microscopy. It can be seen that HCT116 cells treated only with NS-CD and NS-CD/Cys show strong fluorescence in the cytoplasm. Also, the overlay image showed dramatic differences between NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys stimulation. Cell membranes are mostly capable of permeating small-sized molecules and CDs use an endocytosis pathway to enter cells [60–62]. Since CDs can be transferred to cells via endocytosis [63] and it is speculated that CDs entered cells by non-endocytic pathways [64]. In this regard, further investigation is still needed to understand the mechanisms of internalization. In addition, the CDs bound to the membrane translocation peptides can easily overcome the cell membrane barrier, so more CDs can accumulate in the cell to obtain better imaging and improve the efficiency of intracellular labeling [65]. These results suggest that NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys can pass through the cell membrane and enter cells confirming the cellular activity of NS-CDs and NS-CD/Cys as fluorescent probes for biological imaging.

NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys were also used as photoluminescent ink because of the excellent photoluminescent properties (solubility, dispersibility, and low toxicity) [23,24]. The NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys could be used in document writing, security features, printing, and stamps, etc [25]. First, the NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys aqueous solutions were loaded into a clean marker pen without any chemical modification. NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys remained their original photoluminescence on Whatman filter paper after 30 min. of exposure to daylight, thus demonstrating good photostability of NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys [66,67]. Fig. 6 shows the pattern (YU YR-Lee; Yeungnam University, Yong Rok Lee) of written words on the filter paper under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. Therefore, the as-prepared NS-CDs and NS-CDs/Cys have considerable potential for photoluminescence ink pens or anti-counterfeit field real applications.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized excitation-dependent multiple emission nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs). The synthesized NS-CDs were characterized in terms of their chemical composition and structural analysis, confirming the presence of functional groups relevant to sulfur and nitrogen. The resulting NS-CDs and NSCDs/Cys showed excellent PL properties with an increase in quantum yield from 11.4% to 22.5%. These green-emitting NS-CDs were used for highly selective and sensitive Cys detection with a detection limit as low as 23 nM. NS-CDs and NSCDs/Cys were applied successfully to bioimaging in HCT116 cells and Cys detection in aqueous solutions. Therefore, our strategies may provide new insight into the development of a photoluminescent sensor for Cys detection and cellular bioimaging which may help to the diagnosis of diseases associated with Cys concentration in living tissues. Moreover, the use of NS-CDs and NSCDs/Cys as photoluminescent inks in the field of anti-counterfeiting has achieved satisfactory results.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Subramanian Palanisamy: Investigation. Veeraraghavan Vishnu Priya: Formal analysis. Surapaneni Krishna Mohan: Formal analysis. Jae-Jin Shim: Investigation. Khamphone Yelithao: Formal analysis. SangGuan You: Investigation. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
